coredata.dc:title: Investigations on the unique design of heat pipe-assisted thermal management system with novel Chaudhari-Padalkar-Funde number and heat transfer correlations
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Padalkar, Akshay B.
Chaudhari, Mangesh B.
Funde, Adinath M.
coredata.dc:description: The demand for a clean energy storage source in the automobile industry is being fulfilled effectively by the use of electric vehicles (EVs). The most prominent battery technology commercially preferred in EVs is lithium-ion batteries. However, the enormous amount of heat generated while cycling the batteries hinders the overall performance, life cycle, and safety. Therefore, controlling the temperature rise of batteries below the acceptable ranges is vital. Several thermal management techniques are available, but Heat Pipes (HPs) are one of the most efficient and promising technologies. The incorporation of HP in batteries is a difficult task for effective heat extraction. The novelty of the current work lies in the incorporation of HP at central cavities in a cylindrical cell battery, which utilizes the circumferential surface area with the aid of a battery cell contactor. The vertical HP extracts the heat and dissipates it through the top fins. The new non-dimensional number is proposed, named the CPF number (Chaudhari-Padalkar-Funde number) and the heat transfer correlation is developed for the current HP design. The CPF number describes the rate of heat generation to rate of heat dissipation. The physical significance of CPF number reveals the effectiveness of designed TMS. The experimental results reveal that HP with forced convection drops the temperature rise to 51.7 °C with an airflow velocity of 2 ms-1 and up to 47.8 °C with an airflow velocity of 4 ms-1 at a 3C discharge rate. The simulation results of temperature distribution reveal that the peak temperature difference (∆Tmax) within the battery pack is also maintained below 3 °C. Although the mass of the system increased by 18.2 %, the effectiveness of heat extraction increased by 63.5 %. The performance, as analysed by the trade-off score, is 3.32, indicating a highly efficient designed system. The proposed design of a subgroup of 2s2p battery packs is easily replicable to larger-sized cylindrical cell battery packs for EV applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Battery thermal management systems
CPF number (Chaudhari-Padalkar-Funde number)
HP-based battery thermal management system
Natural and forced convection
Battery cell contactor
Battery performance and safety
Cylindrical cell Li-ion battery pack

ABP is thankful to the administrative and technical staff of the Department of Technology and Centre for Energy Studies (Formerly School of Energy Studies), Savitribai Phule Pune University. AMF acknowledges Exide Industries Limited for establishing and using facilities at the EXIDE-SPPU Centre of Excellence in Energy Storage at the Savitribai Phule Pune University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The experimental 2s2p battery packs with different models, (a) without HP, (b) HP with natural convection, (c) HP with forced convection.
Experimental setup diagram with various parts.
Validation of results of experimental and simulation without cooling and HP with natural convection cooling of 2s2p battery pack.
Temperature rise for different rates in HP with natural convection (a) 1C, (b) 1.5C, (c) 2C, and (d) 3C.
Temperature rise for different rates in HP with natural and forced convection with velocities of 2 ms-1 and 4 ms-1, (a) 1C, (b) 1.5C, (c) 2C, and (d) 3C.
HP with natural convection at different discharge rates (a) Temperature rise monitored, (b) Percentage drop in temperature rise.
Comparison of heat transfer correlation with experimental results, (a) Nu vs Re, (b) h vs velocity.

HP with forced convection of airflow velocity of 4 ms-1, (a) Temperature distribution contour at 1C discharge rate, (b) Temperature distribution contour at 2C discharge rate, (c) Temperature distribution contour at 3C discharge rate.

Temperature distribution in forced convection condition at 3C discharge at different parts and locations of (a) 3s3p battery pack with HP cooling, (b) battery cell surface, (c) battery cell contactors, (d) HPs, (e) fins.
Comparison of different literature studies with key results.

row.entry: 57.5 °C (natural convection) 40 °C (MHPA with forced air cooling at fins)
Implementation of MHPA on the side surface of prismatic battery cell for heat extraction able to maintain temperature.
The prismatic battery cell’s flat side is attached to the HP and fins used for forced and natural convection to remove heat.
Heat-pipe applied to the flat side of the prismatic battery cell for effective heat extraction.
To extract heat from the battery cell, a semicircular cell connector is utilized for heat removal.
Cylindrical cell battery pack NMC (2.2 Ah)
A semicircular contactor is used to contact the battery cell surface for heat removal from battery pack.
L-shaped Heat pipe with fins by free and forced convection
51.9 °C (natural convection), 42.2 °C (forced convection with velocity of 2 ms-1)
A l- shaped design of HP extracting the heat from cylindrical cells effectively.
MHPA maintains the temperature rise by extracting heat from flat surface of prismatic cells.
HP with fins on the top cooled by a fan

56.2 °C (HP with natural convection),
48.7 °C (HP with Forced convection 2 ms-1), 43.2 °C (HP with Forced convection 4 ms-1)
The HP extracting the heat by the use of cell contactor utilizing the empty space between the cells and heat removed by fins on top of the HP by natural and forced convection

head.ce:title: Investigations on the unique design of heat pipe-assisted thermal management system with novel Chaudhari-Padalkar-Funde number and heat transfer correlations

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cCentre for Energy Studies (Formerly School of Energy Studies), Savitribai Phule Pune University, Pune, Maharashtra 411007, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The demand for a clean energy storage source in the automobile industry is being fulfilled effectively by the use of electric vehicles (EVs). The most prominent battery technology commercially preferred in EVs is lithium-ion batteries. However, the enormous amount of heat generated while cycling the batteries hinders the overall performance, life cycle, and safety. Therefore, controlling the temperature rise of batteries below the acceptable ranges is vital. Several thermal management techniques are available, but Heat Pipes (HPs) are one of the most efficient and promising technologies. The incorporation of HP in batteries is a difficult task for effective heat extraction. The novelty of the current work lies in the incorporation of HP at central cavities in a cylindrical cell battery, which utilizes the circumferential surface area with the aid of a battery cell contactor. The vertical HP extracts the heat and dissipates it through the top fins. The new non-dimensional number is proposed, named the CPF number (Chaudhari-Padalkar-Funde number) and the heat transfer correlation is developed for the current HP design. The CPF number describes the rate of heat generation to rate of heat dissipation. The physical significance of CPF number reveals the effectiveness of designed TMS. The experimental results reveal that HP with forced convection drops the temperature rise to 51.7 °C with an airflow velocity of 2 ms-1 and up to 47.8 °C with an airflow velocity of 4 ms-1 at a 3C discharge rate. The simulation results of temperature distribution reveal that the peak temperature difference (∆Tmax) within the battery pack is also maintained below 3 °C. Although the mass of the system increased by 18.2 %, the effectiveness of heat extraction increased by 63.5 %. The performance, as analysed by the trade-off score, is 3.32, indicating a highly efficient designed system. The proposed design of a subgroup of 2s2p battery packs is easily replicable to larger-sized cylindrical cell battery packs for EV applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:def-description.ce:para:
Overall heat transfer surface area of the battery pack
[
m
2
]
Average convective heat transfer coefficient [W m-2 K-1]
Volumetric internal heat generation rate [W m-3]
Temperature of the base of the fin [K or °C]
Maximum temperature difference in the battery pack [K or °C]
Mean coefficient of convective heat transfer [W m-2 K-1]
Actual heat transfer rate from the fin [W m-3]
The fin's ideal rate of heat transmission, assuming it is at base temperature throughout [W m-3]
Reversible heat generation due to chemical reactions [W]
The temperature of the fin base [K or °C]
x-coordinate dimensions in the battery pack [m]
y-coordinate dimensions in the battery pack [m]
z-coordinate dimensions in the battery pack [m]
Maximum temperature difference inside the battery pack [K or °C]

ce:section.ce:para: The need of clean and less polluting energy sources has increased enormously. The trend is expected to continue growing to address climate change in the near future. In pursuit of the sustainable development objective, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have gained significant traction as a preferred energy storage solution for both stationary and mobile applications due to their advantageous characteristics [1,2]. The one of the most critical parameter in LIBs is operating temperature, which significantly impacts battery performance, safety, and reduces lifespan, and may also result in a thermal runaway [3,4]. The continuous charge-discharge cycles cause heat production in lithium-ion batteries, which has an adverse effect on performance and safety. Thus, to avoid this, lithium-ion batteries must operate at a temperature between 15 °C and 45 °C, with a peak temperature differential between the battery cells of <5 °C [5–7]. To mitigate the heat generated in LIBs, various prominent thermal management systems (TMS) are classified depending on multiple factors. The most common cooling methods include forced-air cooling, liquid-cooled channels, phase change material (PCM), and heat pipe (HP) cooling [8–10]. The series and parallel battery pack connections also significantly contribute to heat generation in large-format packs and need to be consider for effective TMS [11].

The cylindrical battery cell is one of the most preferred form factors due to its high energy density; Li-ion batteries are perfect for electric vehicle (EV) applications [12]. The battery pack's compactness is a crucial parameter due to space constraints in EV applications. The spacing between battery cells in a battery pack has a considerable impact on the construction of the TMS. Therefore, designing the TMS that retain their portability is a critical task [13,14]. The cylindrical cells generate significant heat because of high energy density which is a primary concern. A circumferential fin utilized the circumferential area of cylindrical battery packs for an effective TMS design [15]. The potential advantages of HPs, namely fast response, durability, low maintenance, and long-lasting operation, are now gaining attention for BTMS in hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs) and electric vehicles (EVs) [16,17].

The HP-assisted TMS offers several advantages, including high extraction, high responsiveness, and efficiency throughout its lifespan, without requiring additional power. Consequently, it exhibits better performance than other TMS [18,19]. The HP technology has commercialization potential for TMS [20]. The HP has proven to be an efficient strategy for keeping both the surface temperature of battery cells and the temperature disparity in battery packs within the specified range limits [21,22]. The HP design is adjustable and flexible to a variety of space constraints. Due to its attractive qualities, it is an excellent choice for cooling HEV/EV batteries [23]. Several research studies used various types of HP for thermal control. The most commonly used design solution is to place the HP beneath the modules or between the cells. Research studies indicate that when used in electric vehicles, HPs can meet industry demands [24,25]. The Flat HP (FHP) consists of fins that remove heat by natural convection, and multiple configurations of FHP are possible to successfully reduce the battery pack's peak temperature as well as temperature uniformity within the battery pack using forced air cooling [26]. According to the authors, using an HP in conjunction with a heat sink is the most effective cooling technique for maintaining battery operating temperatures below 50 °C, while also reducing heat sink resistance by 30 %. Water-cooled HPs are also good at controlling battery surface temperature and keeping it below the acceptable range of 55 °C [27].

According to the authors, natural convection is insufficient to maintain the battery's temperature stability at high discharge rates. By utilizing natural convection able to keep the battery temperature below 40 °C at a discharge rate of 1C, the Micro-HP Array (MHPA) eliminates the need for an additional air-cooling device. The battery temperature can be maintained at the 3C discharge rate by providing an airflow of 3 ms⁻¹ [28]. The authors proposed a battery cell integrated with various cooling modes, achieving peak temperatures and percentage drops in temperature of up to 49 °C and 13.7 % under natural convection conditions, and 38.8 °C and 31.6 % under HP cooling [29]. The parameters, such as location, heat sink size, mode of cooling, and type, significantly impacted HP-based system performance. The authors investigated HP incorporating fins cooled via forced convection both experimentally and numerically. The findings revealed that natural convection alone is insufficient. Thus, with a discharge rate of 2C, using an air circulation velocity of 4 ms⁻¹ keeps the battery pack's peak temperature below 40 °C [30]. The numerical analysis used for flat plate HP in various configurations was connected to a single cell. In the forced convection condition, the highest temperatures of battery cells were around 35 °C at the 2C discharge rate [31]. The HP with air cooling used a novel interconnection that links the cylindrical battery cell to the HP. A copper sheet was used to supplement. The cylindrical battery cell's semi-circumferential curve connects the HP to fins that are coupled to the condenser and ventilated. Analyzing numerous configurations, it was determined that positioning the HPs at the center of the battery pack was the most effective option. The result found that the battery pack's peak temperature was reduced by 15 °C compared with natural convection [32]. The study shows that employing a 3 ms-1 air velocity at 3C discharge, the Micro-HP Array (MHPA) as the BTMS is capable of keeping a temperature below 40 °C at 1 °C discharge in both forced and natural airflow [33].

HPs and air cooling were used to cool the 24-cell battery pack. Compared to natural convection, the results show that using HPs, air cooling, and HPs with copper sheets can dramatically lower peak pack temperatures by up to 34.5 %, 42.1 %, and 42.7 %, respectively [34]. HP-based TMS techniques with various structural combinations are employed to maintain the maximum temperature and temperature consistency within the designated range. An HP and a cold plate (CP) make up the suggested battery cooling structure. At a discharge rate of 2C, the HP-CP arrangement can sustain the battery's maximum temperature and temperature uniformity by 34.1 °C and 1 °C, respectively [35]. The authors devised a TMS that included a cooling spray and an HP. The findings show that even at a high discharge rate of about 2C, it can effectively decrease the battery pack's highest temperature by 29.2 °C compared to a battery pack with an uncooled condition [36]. The proposed passive TMS technology for EVs includes HP and phase change material (PCM). The peak temperature of the cells was reduced by 17.3 % and 40.7 %, respectively, when using an HP cooling technique and a PCM-augmented HP cooling approach compared to natural convection [37]. A peak temperature of 50 °C was successfully maintained at a discharge rate of 3C by combining liquid cooling and HP techniques. Despite having a discharge rate of 3C, the PCM/HP-Liquid mixture exhibited a clearly sustained temperature fluctuation of approximately 3 °C [38]. To create hybrid TMS that efficiently lower and regulate battery temperatures, HP is combined with other TMS technologies. The key parameters reported in the literature and comarison with reported ones is given in Table 1.

The apparent gaps in previously reported in the above studies were overcome in the present study. The current study aims to effectively extract heat from cylindrical battery cells while maintaining the compactness of the modules. Additionally, the efficient use of a cell contactor in conjunction with an HP can extract heat from the battery pack. With the use of a cell contactor, an HP draws heat from battery cells and transmits it to fins for dissipation through natural and forced convection. The battery cell contactor provides firm contact with the cell body, facilitated by thermal paste, which enables efficient heat transfer to the HP. The experiments and simulations are designed for various cases of HP cooling for a 2s2p battery pack. The results of natural convection and forced convection are analysed to compare the impact of air cooling. Non-dimensional analysis is performed [40] to develop a heat transfer correlation using experimental results, and a new non-dimensional number viz the CPF number (Chaudhari-Padalkar-Funde number), is proposed. The CPF number (Chaudhari-Padalkar-Funde) gives significance of discharge timescale and diffusion timescale. The experimental and modelling findings clearly reveals that keeping the temperature within the specified limits is crucial performance and safety. The simulation results of a large-format 3s3p battery pack with a subgroup of 2s2p clearly depict that the proposed design can be applicable to higher capacity cylindrical cell EV battery packs.

Firstly, all parts of the HP-based TMS are assembled to the cylindrical battery cells, which are then connected to form a 2s2p battery pack. The battery cell contactor is attached to the battery cells and HP firmly by using thermal grease for a tight contact. The HP design consists of a battery cell contactor, an HP, and fins. The circumferential fins are mounted on the HP for heat dissipation by natural and forced convection. The battery pack with an HP setup is charged-discharged using the battery cycler at various rates, from 0.5C to 3C. The charge-discharge program is given by using the cycler software. The constant current and constant voltage (CCCV) method is used for charging, while the constant current (CC) method is used for discharging. The various parameters, including voltage, current, and temperature, are monitored during cycling. The calibrated temperature sensors are placed at multiple locations, including the battery cell surface, battery cell contactor, bottom fins, and top fins, to monitor heat dissipation from the battery cells to the fins. The schematic of the experimental setup, including all its components, is shown in Fig. 2. These components comprise the battery cycler, data logger, temperature sensors, battery tab connections, anemometer, fan, airflow channel, and other elements. The temperature at the surface of the battery cell is monitored every 30 s, and temperature rises at multiple locations are recorded by using a data logger. The AC fan used for airflow has a specification of 240 V and 0.1 A, which gives a maximum airflow of 6 ms⁻¹. The airflow velocity is varied by using a dimmer stat, which adjusts the voltage supplied. An anemometer is used to measure the velocity of airflow. The temperature rise of the battery pack is monitored and recorded using six calibrated PT-100 temperature sensors. Temperature sensors are employed, and data is collected using a logger. The experimental measurements were recorded at an ambient temperature of approximately 30 °C, maintained in an air-conditioned environment. The air-cooling channel and anemometer are used for airflow measurement. Because of surface area of cylindrical battery cells are difficult to incorporate the heat pipe. However, the use of cell contactor helps to cover the circumferential area of cylindrical battery cells and connect with the HP pipe to extract the generated heat effectively. Also, use of thermal paste for tight contact for heat extraction. The 2s2p battery pack, featuring a battery cell contactor and measurement of airflow velocity using an anemometer, is shown in the supplementary files (Fig. S2 and Fig. S3). The forced convection setup with an airflow channel is illustrated in the supplementary file (Fig. S4). The temperature rise at different locations, as recorded by temperature sensors at the battery cell surface, at the cell contactor, at the bottom fin on the HP, and the top fin on the HP, is shown in the supplementary files, Figs. S5 and S6. The LFP (Lithium Ferro-Phosphate) with a form factor of 32700 battery cells is used in the experimental setup. The technical specifications of battery cells and packs are described in Table 2 .
HP is fast and responsive in heat extraction and dissipation. The HP specifications are given in Table 3 :

The most significant measurement error in the experimental measures, which is well within the measurement's uncertainty, is less than ±3 %. The uncertainty in the experimentation found to be 1.253 %. The measurement errors are listed in Table 4 .

The different cases are designed to analyze the impact of the HP-based TMS for the battery pack. The designed instances are described in Fig. 1
.
The 2s2p battery pack without HP cooling undergoes charge-discharge cycles at various rates in ambient environments of approximately 30 °C.

It comprises a 2s2p battery pack with an HP centered between the four battery cells, using a battery cell contactor to link the battery cell's circumferential area to the HP. The vertical heat sink consists of circumferential fins mounted on an HP. The top side of the HP features 30 circumferential fins, each with an outward diameter of 20 mm, an inner diameter of 8 mm, a thickness of 0.7 mm, and a 2 mm spacing between fins.
The complete HP design with cell contactor and fins incorporated with a 2s2p battery and the addition of airflow velocity of 2 ms-1 and 4 ms-1 is given in the inlet section of the design.

The ANSYS FLUENT software is used to simulate the battery pack. There are various methods to follow when designing a battery model in the software. The first step is to create a 3D model of the battery cells, including their negative and positive zones, battery tabs, and busbars for connections. The LFP cell for the battery has an output of 6 Ah and a voltage of 3.2 V. The battery cells are connected in parallel and series to form a 2s2p battery pack with a total capacity of 12 Ah and an ideal operating voltage of 6.4 V. The transient model is used in conjunction with the Dual Potential Multi-Scale Multi-Domain (MSMD) and Equivalent Circuit Model (ECM), which both utilize k-epsilon viscous turbulence modelling to enhance airflow in the battery pack. The Y+ value obtained from the turbulent numerical model ranges between 24.63 and 33.09. Air serves as the coolant fluid, and the module is subject to intake and outflow boundary limits. The transient analysis was chosen since the rates of discharge are time-dependent. In the computational model, HP properties are used as a high-conducting material that transfers heat to the heat sink. The HP in the computational model is used as a high-heat-conducting material with a conductivity coefficient of 393 W/(m·K), which conducts heat towards the heat sink [41,42].

The second phase involves meshing, and the size of the mesh is a crucial component in improving the accuracy of the results. Changing the highest possible element size dramatically changes the total number of elements and nodes. A tiny grid size and hex‑dominant mesh enhance the accuracy of the results. The small element size provides more nodes for a precise analysis of the heat-generating hotspot, while skewness adds nodes and components at the curvature. The temperature rise-related component sizes of 1 mm, 2 mm, and 4 mm indicate that, up to a component size of 1 mm, the total number of elements is exceptionally high, yet it provides outstanding accuracy of results. The temperature rise results fluctuate according to the element size, ranging from 4 mm to 1 mm; however, below 1 mm, the temperature rise results vary negligibly. The optimized element size of 1 mm should be selected for optimal computational time and better results. The number of nodes and elements is compared with the maximum element size of the mesh for the independence study shown in Table 5 .

The isothermal condition with an ambient temperature of approximately 30 °C or 303 K allows a convective heat transfer coefficient to be taken as h = 5 to 9 W/m²K1 [43–45] for natural convection. The inlet airflow velocity of air is 2 ms-1, and 4 ms-1 is applied to forced convection. The various discharge rates, ranging from 0.5C to 3C, were applied to the battery pack. According to the manufacturer's specifications, the minimum cell voltage for a single battery cell is 2.5 V, and the maximum voltage is 4.2 V. The Battery Current given according to discharge cycles serves as the criterion for convergence. In the transient analysis, the time is specified according to the discharge time, such as 7200 s, 3600 s, 1800 s, and 1200 s. The tracking of numerical convergence is achieved by checking the reduction in residuals, and the adaptive time-stepping adjusts the temperature rise if the residuals stagnate. The other parameters, such as track battery voltage, temperature rise, and grid/ time step independence study.

The battery cell body treated as active materials and tab bodies are treated as passive materials in modelling. The material properties of the battery's active material, passive material, and tab material are given in Table 6 :

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of the setup for the solver
ce:section.ce:para: The model is designed to accommodate different boundary conditions. The charge-discharge cycles of the batteries generate heat, leading to a temperature rise. Heat generation increases with larger discharge rates, and thus the battery temperature rises as well. This battery model incorporates an HP, a battery cell contactor, and a heat sink. The various results are analyzed at the output of the simulation. The different computational conditions are tested by comparing them to the experimental method. The models without cooling are compared with the HP cooling models.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of experimental data with simulation data
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental and modelling results are validated for various discharge rates. The simulations are carried out using the ANSYS FLUENT program, and the output results are obtained at the end of the computation. The temperature increase at the conclusion of discharge cycles is measured for two different versions of 2s2p battery packs, one with and one without HP cooling. Fig. 3 illustrates the comparative findings of the temperature rise in experiments and simulations. The percentage difference in the increase in temperature results between an experiment and a simulation was 3.95 %. The error rate, expressed as a percentage, is obtained within the desired boundaries to be used in subsequent calculations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental results for the HP cooling
ce:section.ce:para: The 2s2p battery pack is tested in a variety of settings, including no cooling, natural convection, and forced convection at a velocity of 2 ms-1. The temperature rise charts are compared at discharge rates ranging from 1C to 3C. The Fig. 4 depicts the temperature rise of battery pack at 1C, 1.5C, 2C, and 3C discharge rates decreased up to 44.1 °C, 51.8 °C, 59.5 °C and 78.9 °C in the without HP cooling applied, drops up to 38.8 °C, 43.8 °C, 47.7 °C and 56.2 °C in the case of HP with natural convection and drops up to 35.3 °C, 40.3 °C, 42.3 °C, and 48.7 °C in the case of HP with airflow velocity of 2 ms-1. The temperature of the battery pack significantly drops by the incorporation of HP with comparison to without HP cooling. The HP extracts the generated heat from the battery cells with the help of the cell contactor and removes it through the fins.

The results of temperature rise compared with conditions without cooling, HP with natural convection, and HP with airflow velocity of 2 ms-1. The temperature rise drops by the implementation of the HP design with effective heat removal compared to the uncooled condition. It is observed that the temperature rise is at a higher rate in the first and last 20 % of SOC compared to the middle 60 % of SOC at a lower discharge rate. While at a higher discharge rate, there is a steep increase in temperature. The results reveals that incorporation HP effectively extracting the heat and maintain the temperature in the desired limits from complex cell bodies in concise space. HP can be employed in large format battery packs for heat extraction in the complex battery packs

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of airflow velocity on temperature rise of battery pack
ce:section.ce:para: The effectiveness of forced air cooling was analysed by varying airflow velocities of 2 ms-1 and 4 ms-1 compared with natural convection conditions. The analysis of airflow rate optimizes power consumption due to air cooling. Fig. 5 shows that using HP at forced convection and natural convection, with airflow velocities of 2 ms-1 and 4 ms-1, efficiently lowers the battery pack's temperature. The airflow velocity significantly contributes to the heat sink's enhanced heat dissipation, thereby reducing the battery pack's overall temperature rise. Fig. 5 displays the surface temperature of the battery pack as it changes with discharge time. The temperature of battery pack at 1C, 1.5C, 2C, and 3C discharge rates decreased from 38.8 °C, 43.8 °C, 47.7 °C and 56.2 °C in the case of natural convection to 35.3 °C, 40.3 °C, 42.3 °C, and 48.7 °C in the case of HP with airflow velocity of 2 ms-1 and to 33.6 °C, 35.6 °C, 38.7 °C, and 43.2 °C in the case of HP with airflow velocity of 4 ms-1.

The results depict that the airflow velocity is also a crucial parameter should be optimised for effective heat extraction. However, the energy consumption of the fan should be optimised for efficient BTMS. The velocity of the airflow can be varied as per the need of cooling load will be excellent for optimised power consumption. The cooling fluid for heat extraction from the fins also be critical parameter in HP based BTMS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the drop in temperature rise at different configurations
ce:section.ce:para: The overall temperature rise is monitored at the end of the discharge cycle. The without-cooling condition is compared with HP cooling using natural convection and forced convection, as well as the forced air-cooling case, at airflow velocities of 2 ms-1 and 4 ms-1. The results in Fig. 6 (a) depict the comparative temperature of the battery pack for the designed cases. It reveals that the developed HP design effectively dropped and maintained the temperature of the battery pack. In comparison to without cooling conditions at 3C, discharge rate, the temperature of the battery pack dropped from 78.9 °C to 56.2 °C in the case of natural convection, 48.7 °C in the case of HP with forced air cooling at 2 ms-1 and 43.2 °C in case of HP with forced air cooling at 4 ms-1. The results shown in Fig. 6(b) indicate that the percentage drop in temperature rise for the HP case with natural convection ranges from 37 % to 47 %. The results show that the percentage drop in temperature rise for HP with forced convection ranges from 58 % to 75 %. The percentage drop is lower in lower discharges because of lesser heat generation compared to higher discharge rates. A significant outcome is that natural convection can also reduce the temperature by around 40 % with a compact fin design, resulting in a smaller space requirement. However, forced convection is advantageous for faster heat removal and maintaining the temperature below 45 °C, even at a 3C discharge rate. However, airflow velocity is optimized to minimize power consumption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heat transfer correlation for battery pack using HP design
ce:section.ce:para: The heat transfer correlation depends on various parameters related to an HP with circumferential fins and air cooling. The forced air-cooling parameter is considered.

The heat transfer coefficient depends on various factors such as the characteristic length (L), thermal conductivity (k), dynamic viscosity (µ), specific heat (Cp), density (ρ), velocity of airflow (V), and discharge rate (C)
(1)
h = f ( L , μ , ρ , k , V ,
C p
, C )
Where discharge rate (C) is expressed as
(2)
C =
C u r r e n t
d r a w n
i n
A m p e r e
,
C a p a c i t y
o f
b a t t e r y
i n
A m p e r e − h o u r

Using Buckingham's π theorem, four π terms are identified as given below.
(3)
Q
c o n v
= f
(
L , ρ ,
μ ,
k ,
V , C p ,
h , C
)

By applying dimensionless analysis
(4)
Π
1
=
ρ V L
μ
= R e
(5)

=

k
= P r
(6)

=

= N u
(7)

v

)

Where, Re is the Reynolds number, Pr is the Prandtl number, Nu is the Nusselt number, and CPF is the Chaudhari-Padalkar-Funde number
(8)
CPF =
H e a t
g e n e r a t i o n
t i m e

t i m e

(

2
)

The following four non-dimensional parameters can be obtained by condensing these eight-dimensional parameters:
(9)
h L
k
= f
(
ρ v
L
2

μ C p

,
C

2
)
)

Therefore, governed by the following non-dimensional groups:
(10)
N u = f
(
R e ,
P r ,
C P F
)
(11)
N u = p × R
e
x
× P
r
y
× C P
F
z

The CPF number dependent on characteristic length (L), discharge rate (key parameter for heat generation) of battery (C) and kinematic viscosity (v). The CPF number defined as rate of heat generation with respect to rate of heat dissipation. If heat generation is equal to heat dissipation the CPF number tends to unity. The physical significance of CPF number describes the effectiveness of designed TMS. The CPF number should be considered for optimised design of TMS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Correlation for HP with forced convection TMS
ce:section.ce:para:
Correlations between fin thickness, airflow velocity, and fin count are developed using multiple regression analysis. The variation in the average Nusselt number, as an estimation of Reynolds' number (Re), can be explained by the following equation, where the value of the Prandtl number (Pr) is based on forced convection, and the Discharge rate coefficient (Cd) is used. Discharge rate is from 0.5C to 3C and airflow velocity is 1 ms-1 to 6 ms-1
(12)
N u = 0.1053
× R
e
0.618
× P
r
0.33
×
C P
F
0.1
Where values are in the ranges,

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of results of correlation and experimental method
ce:section.ce:para: The outcomes of Nusselt number (Nu) and convective heat transfer coefficient (h) are compared for experimental and correlation methods. A regression analysis was conducted to compare the experimental and correlation results. Nusselt number and convective transfer increase with an increase in airflow velocity, but the rate slightly decreases with increasing airflow rate, as described in Fig. 7 (a) and (b). The Reynolds number rises linearly with airflow velocity. The discovered correlation aids in calculating heat transfer parameters for HP having circumferential fins and air cooling. The rate of discharge and airflow velocity have a significant impact on heat transfer in HP-based TMS. The total error between the experiment and correlation results is <4 %.

The simulation conducted for the 2s2p battery pack reveals different discharge rates at an airflow velocity of 4 ms-1. The temperature distribution contours, which include the HP, are shown at multiple locations within the battery pack. In Fig. 8 , temperature rise contours clearly show that heat produced from battery cells is extracted by the cell contactor and transmitted to the HP for dissipation through fins. The temperature rise varies at different locations due to heat transmission, which is effectively carried out by the HP, and temperature contours illustrate the heat transfer process. The temperature rise was maintained at approximately 34.4 °C at 1C, 43.01 °C at 2C, and 48.5 °C at 3C discharge rates. The temperature uniformity results reveal that the temperature difference (∆T) within the battery pack is also maintained below 3 °C even at a 3C discharge rate, which is below the desired limit of 5 °C. The HP absorbs the heat through the battery cell contactor and transmits it towards the fins for dissipation. The results of temperature contours clearly indicate significant cooling by the HP from the cell contactor to the fins.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HP design for 3s3p large battery pack
ce:section.ce:para: The experiments are conducted for a 2s2p battery pack. Therefore, the replicability of the current design is simulated for a 3s3p battery pack with four HPs and four cell contactors. For different discharge rates, the temperature rise of the battery pack is tracked. Nonetheless, Fig. 9 displays the temperature profile at various points on the battery pack, which was discharged at a rate of 3C. Potent heat transfer through battery cells to fins is indicated by temperature monitoring at the battery cell surface, contactor, HPs, and fins. The temperature contours show that the temperature rise is maintained around 52.5 °C even at 3C discharge with an airflow velocity of 4 ms-1.

The temperature contours clearly show that HP is able to extract and dissipate the heat generated by the battery cells and maintain the battery temperature within the desired limits. Therefore, the current design is easily replicable for larger battery packs, and it is more efficient for larger battery packs because the consecutive HPs extract heat from nearby cells. The simulation results of a large-format 3s3p battery pack with a subgroup of 2s2p clearly depict that the proposed design should be applied to cylindrical cell EV battery packs.

The current HP-based design consists of parts such as the battery cell contactor, HP, and fins, which increase the system's weight. The battery pack has a high energy density; therefore, the system's mass is already heavier. The mass increase and compactness of battery packs are critical parameters. The current HP design increases the mass and percentage rise in weight, as shown in Table 7 .

The trade-off analysis between thermal benefits, weight, and energy density can be expressed using a multi-optimization framework. The trade-off score is calculated for 2S2P battery pack results, including temperature rise at a 3C discharge rate and HP with forced convection.
(13)
Trade −
off
Score
=
Tmax
reduction
(
10 ∘
C
)
− 0.5
(
Δ
m
2 kg
)
− 0.3
(
Δ
E density
50 Wh / kg
)
Trade-off Score = 3.32

The goal of a current study is to keep the battery temperature within the intended ranges. The number of analyses included the rise in battery pack temperature, airflow velocity, and heat transfer correlation. The main findings of the current investigation are as follows:
•
The proposed HP design efficiently extracts heat for a subgroup of 2s2p battery packs and can be easily replicated for large-size battery packs.
•
The current HP design can maintain the battery pack's surface temperature at 56.2 °C in natural convection, 48.7 °C in HP with forced convection at a velocity of 2 ms-1, and 43.2 °C in HP with forced air-cooling velocity of 4 ms-1, even at a 3C discharge rate.
•
The results of the percentage rise in the drop in the outer temperature of the battery pack compared to the condition without cooling are 46.4 % in natural convection, 61.8 % in HP with forced air colling velocity of 2 ms-1, and 73.1 % HP with forced air colling velocity of 4 ms-1, even at a 3C discharge rate.
•
HP with natural convection can be used for lower discharge rates, but HP with forced convection is preferred for higher discharge rates and better cooling performance.
•
Although the mass of the system increases by 18.2 %, the effectiveness of heat extraction rises by 63.5 %. The performance improvement calculated by the trade-off score of 3.32, which indicates a high efficacy of the designed system.
•
Simulation findings for the 3s3p battery pack using HP cooling show that even at a 3C discharge rate, the temperature rise is maintained at approximately 52.5 °C, and the battery pack's temperature uniformity (∆T) remains below 3 °C.
•
The proposed design consists of a subgroup of 2s2p battery packs, which can be easily replicated to large-format cylindrical cell battery packs for EV applications.
•
Heat transfer correlation with a non-dimensional CPF (Chaudhari-Padalkar-Funde) number is proposed. The CPF number defined as rate of heat generation with respect to rate of heat dissipation. If heat generation is equal to heat dissipation the CPF number tends to unity. The physical significance of CPF number describes the effectiveness of designed TMS. The CPF number should be considered for optimised design of TMS. The limitations are basically cost of TMS and optimisation of parameters in HP based BTMS.

In the future prospects the optimising HP design enhances the cooling efficiency of HP-based TMS. The maintaining the temperatures at extreme positive and negative ambient conditions are the key challenge particularly in the industrial battery packs.

Akshay B. Padalkar: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Mangesh B. Chaudhari: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Conceptualization. Adinath M. Funde: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors state that they have no known competing financial interests or personal ties that might have influenced the research presented in this study.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: ABP acknowledges Chhatrapati Shahu Maharaj Research, Training and Human Development Institute (SARTHI), Pune, Government of Maharashtra, for giving me the CSMNRF-2022 Fellowship. ABP is thankful to the administrative and technical staff of the Department of Technology and Centre for Energy Studies (Formerly School of Energy Studies), Savitribai Phule Pune University. AMF acknowledges Exide Industries Limited for establishing and using facilities at the EXIDE-SPPU Centre of Excellence in Energy Storage at the Savitribai Phule Pune University.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.rineng.2025.106327.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: PCM assisted heat pipe cooling system for the thermal management of an LTO cell for high-current profiles, Case Stud
Therm. Eng.
